content.text: What It Is

Quantitative launch criteria are specific, measurable thresholds that a GenAI feature or agentic system must satisfy before it is permitted to ship to production. Unlike qualitative readiness checks — "the output feels right" or "engineers are comfortable" — quantitative criteria produce a binary gate: the measured value either clears the threshold or it does not.

A threshold definition has five parts: (1) the metric name and its operational definition, (2) the measurement method and data source, (3) the numerical boundary (the threshold itself), (4) the gate type — hard or soft — and (5) the minimum sample size required for the measurement to be statistically valid. A threshold missing any of these parts is incomplete and cannot function as a real gate.

The criteria are authored before evaluation begins. Writing a threshold after seeing the data inverts the scientific process and enables implicit p-hacking — teams that iterate on their criteria until the system passes have not actually passed anything. For this reason, launch criteria documents must be timestamped, version-controlled, and approved by stakeholders before the evaluation window opens.

For agentic AI specifically — systems that plan, use tools, and execute multi-step workflows — quantitative criteria address five categories: quality (does the agent succeed at the task?), performance (how fast and at what throughput?), reliability (how often does it fail?), safety (does it generate harmful or policy-violating content, and can it resist adversarial prompts?), and cost (what is the per-query expenditure in tokens and dollars?).
Why It Matters

AI systems fail in ways that are fundamentally different from traditional software bugs. A conventional service either returns the correct result or raises an exception. A language model returns a plausible-sounding result that may be factually wrong, stylistically harmful, or operationally inefficient — often without any error signal to the caller. This makes subjective readiness checks insufficient at scale.

Without quantitative thresholds, teams face three failure modes. The first is launch creep: the system is perpetually "almost ready" because there is no objective definition of ready. The second is blind confidence: engineers feel good about the system because they have been iterating on it for weeks, which introduces familiarity bias. The third is post-launch surprise: metrics that were never measured before launch reveal themselves in production through user complaints.

Quantitative criteria also provide accountability and auditability. When a regulator, an enterprise customer, or an internal risk committee asks "what evidence did you have that this system was safe before you deployed it?", a signed threshold document with measured results is the answer. Verbal assurances do not survive audits.

For agentic systems that act in the world — calling APIs, writing files, sending emails, executing code — the stakes are higher than for read-only recommendation engines. A miscalibrated agent can take irreversible actions at machine speed. Quantitative criteria create a forcing function to measure, document, and attest to system behavior before those consequences can occur at scale.
Benefits
Objective go/no-go decisions.
Removes the social pressure to ship because the team is tired of iterating. If the threshold is not met, the ship does not go. This removes ambiguity from launch meetings.
Early detection of regressions.
When criteria are re-evaluated after every model upgrade, prompt change, or retrieval re-index, regressions are caught before they reach users.
Prioritization signal.
A threshold that repeatedly fails tells engineering exactly where to invest. Without it, improvement efforts diffuse across the system.
Audit and compliance support.
Regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal) need evidence of pre-launch validation. Threshold documents serve as that evidence.
Cross-team alignment.

Product, engineering, legal, and safety teams often have different intuitions about readiness. A shared threshold document forces those intuitions into numbers and surfaces disagreements before launch, not after.
Versioned baselines.
Criteria from v1 become the minimum bar for v2. Over time you accumulate a performance history that reveals whether the system is improving.
Trade-offs

Quantitative criteria create rigor, but they also create overhead and risk when misapplied. Understanding the trade-offs prevents the process from becoming a bureaucratic ritual that delays launches without actually improving safety.
Threshold gaming.

Once engineers know the exact threshold, they may optimize specifically for the test set rather than the underlying capability. Mitigate by rotating evaluation sets and not publishing the exact test cases.
False precision.

A threshold of 90.0% accuracy sounds precise, but if the evaluation set has 100 items, the 95% confidence interval spans roughly ±6 percentage points. Claiming that a system "meets the 90% threshold" on 100 samples is statistically weak.
Metric fixation.

A system can pass every quantitative gate and still be a poor product. Criteria capture what you measure; they do not capture what you have not thought to measure. Pair quantitative gates with lightweight human evaluation.
Cost of measurement.
Generating statistically valid samples and running human evaluation for each is expensive. For small teams this can dominate iteration cycle time. Prioritize hard gates; make soft gates lightweight.
Threshold staleness.

A threshold calibrated against year-old usage patterns may be too lax once the feature reaches 10x the original user volume. Criteria must be reviewed when context changes — new use cases, new user segments, new risk surfaces.
How It Works
The process runs in four phases: definition, baselining, evaluation, and gate decision.
Phase 1 — Definition.

Before any model is trained or any prompt is authored, stakeholders agree on which metrics matter for this use case and what numerical values constitute acceptable behavior. These values are stored in a version-controlled criteria document and signed off by product, engineering, safety, and legal.
Phase 2 — Baselining.

The system is run in shadow mode or dark launch — receiving production traffic but not surfacing results to users. This generates real-distribution samples. The baseline measurements from this phase inform whether the pre-set thresholds are calibrated correctly. If the baseline shows an existing human-handled process achieves 75% accuracy, setting a threshold of 95% for the AI system is aspirational, not achievable. The threshold is adjusted upward from the baseline but constrained by business requirements.
Phase 3 — Evaluation.

The system is evaluated against a held-out test set that was not used during development. Evaluations for accuracy and hallucination rate typically require human raters; evaluations for latency, error rate, and cost can be automated. Results are recorded with confidence intervals, not just point estimates.
Phase 4 — Gate decision.

Each metric is compared against its threshold. Hard gates that fail block launch unconditionally. Soft gates that fail require a documented risk-acceptance memo from the product owner. If any hard gate fails, the team enters a remediation cycle and re-evaluates after the fix is deployed. The decision — go, no-go, or conditional — is recorded along with the measured values and the rationale.
Architecture
A launch-criteria evaluation system has several components that work together. The diagram below shows a typical threshold dashboard with traffic-light status for multiple metric categories.

attrs.alt: Go/No-Go Launch Criteria Dashboard showing quality, performance, reliability, and safety metric status with traffic-light indicators

content.text: The evaluation pipeline consists of four interconnected subsystems. The test harness runs the model against a curated evaluation dataset and records raw outputs. The measurement layer computes metrics from those outputs — some automatically (latency, error rate, token count) and some via human rater panels (accuracy, hallucination, harm). The threshold comparator checks each measured value against its defined gate value and emits a pass/warning/fail status. The reporting layer assembles the per-metric statuses into a launch decision artifact that stakeholders sign.

A critical architectural decision is whether the evaluation pipeline is integrated into CI/CD or runs as a separate manual gate. For small teams, a manual gate run before each release is tractable. For teams shipping multiple times per week, the evaluation must be automated — but automated accuracy and hallucination scoring requires an LLM-as-judge setup, which itself introduces measurement uncertainty that must be accounted for.
When to Use It
Quantitative launch criteria are appropriate whenever an AI system is customer-facing, drives consequential decisions, or generates content that users will trust. Specifically:
Any production launch
of a GenAI feature, regardless of scope. Even internal tools benefit from criteria, because internal users trust AI outputs with high fidelity.
Major model upgrades
(e.g., switching from one foundation model version to another). Model providers change behavior between versions; re-evaluation ensures the new model still meets the bar.
Significant prompt changes
— especially changes to the system prompt, tool definitions, or few-shot examples for an agentic system.
Retrieval corpus updates
in RAG systems. Adding or removing large document sets changes what the model "knows" and can shift accuracy and hallucination rates substantially.
Scope expansions
— e.g., when an agentic system that previously only read data is granted write permissions, or when a customer support bot is extended to handle billing disputes in addition to technical questions.
Quantitative criteria are less critical — though still useful as documentation — for entirely internal proof-of-concept work where no real user data is processed and no production actions are taken.
How to Implement
Step 1: Define the metric taxonomy.

For each metric, write its operational definition unambiguously. "Accuracy" is underspecified. "Fraction of responses rated 4 or 5 on a 5-point correctness rubric by two independent human raters, with inter-rater agreement required" is a definition.
Step 2: Run a dark launch to establish baseline.

Shadow the production system for two to four weeks. Log every request and your system's generated response (do not show it to users). From this log, draw a stratified sample covering the full distribution of request types. Measure baseline performance. This anchors your thresholds in reality.
Step 3: Set thresholds from three inputs.

First, the baseline — the threshold should be meaningfully above what the existing process delivers, or above what a simple non-AI baseline achieves (e.g., a keyword search). Second, industry benchmarks published by vendors and research papers for similar use cases. Third, business requirements — if customer support SLA mandates first-contact resolution in 95% of cases, your task completion threshold must be at least 95%.
Step 4: Classify each threshold as hard or soft.

Hard gates block launch outright if not met. Soft gates generate a warning that requires documented risk acceptance. Safety metrics (harm rate, jailbreak resistance) are always hard gates. Business-critical quality metrics (task completion rate for an autonomous agent) are typically hard gates. Secondary quality metrics (tone appropriateness, formatting consistency) can be soft gates.
Step 5: Determine sample sizes for statistical validity.
Use the Wilson score interval formula for proportions. For a target threshold of p₀ with desired margin of error ε at confidence level z (z=1.96 for 95%):

For a threshold of 90% with a desired ±3% margin of error at 95% confidence: n ≥ (1.96² × 0.9 × 0.1) / 0.03² ≈ 384 samples. This is the minimum. For tail metrics like harm rate (target ≤0.1%), you need dramatically larger samples: detecting a 0.1% rate with ±0.05% margin requires n ≈ 15,000 samples.
Step 6: Author the criteria document and get sign-off.

The document is timestamped, stored in version control, and approved before evaluation begins. Approval signatures from product owner, engineering lead, safety lead, and legal (for regulated industries) are recorded.
Step 7: Execute evaluation and record results.

Run the test harness against the held-out evaluation set. Record point estimates and confidence intervals for every metric. Attach evaluation set provenance (hash of the dataset) to ensure reproducibility.
Step 8: Gate decision meeting.

Stakeholders review the results document. Hard gate failures trigger a no-go. The remediation plan is documented: which metric failed, what engineering work will address it, and when the next evaluation window opens.
Examples
Below is a YAML threshold definition file for an agentic customer support system. This format is directly parseable by an evaluation harness that reads thresholds at runtime.

# launch-criteria.yaml
# Feature: Customer Support AI Agent v2.1
# Authored: 2026-07-10
# Approved-by: [product-owner, eng-lead, safety-lead]
# Evaluation-window-opens: 2026-07-14

feature:
  name: "Customer Support AI Agent"
  version: "2.1.0"
  use_case: "customer_support"

evaluation:
  dataset: "eval/customer-support-2026-q3.jsonl"
  dataset_sha256: "a3f9c12b..."
  min_sample_size: 500
  confidence_level: 0.95

criteria:
  quality:
    - metric: task_completion_rate
      description: "Fraction of sessions where the user's issue was fully resolved without escalation"
      measurement: "human_rater_panel_2"
      threshold: 0.90
      direction: gte
      gate_type: hard
      warning_band: 0.05  # warn if within 5pp of threshold

    - metric: answer_accuracy
      description: "Fraction of factual claims rated correct by subject-matter expert raters"
      measurement: "sme_rater_panel_2"
      threshold: 0.85
      direction: gte
      gate_type: hard
      warning_band: 0.03

    - metric: hallucination_rate
      description: "Fraction of responses containing at least one verifiably false claim"
      measurement: "human_rater_panel_2_with_source_verification"
      threshold: 0.03
      direction: lte
      gate_type: hard
      warning_band: 0.01

    - metric: csat_score
      description: "Mean post-session user satisfaction rating on 1-5 scale"
      measurement: "post_session_survey"
      threshold: 4.0
      direction: gte
      gate_type: soft
      warning_band: 0.3

  performance:
    - metric: p50_latency_seconds
      description: "Median end-to-end response latency from request receipt to first token"
      measurement: "automated_load_test"
      threshold: 2.0
      direction: lte
      gate_type: soft

    - metric: p95_latency_seconds
      description: "95th-percentile end-to-end response latency"
      measurement: "automated_load_test"
      threshold: 5.0
      direction: lte
      gate_type: hard

    - metric: p99_latency_seconds
      description: "99th-percentile end-to-end response latency"
      measurement: "automated_load_test"
      threshold: 10.0
      direction: lte
      gate_type: hard

    - metric: throughput_rps
      description: "Maximum sustained requests per second at p95 latency threshold"
      measurement: "automated_load_test"
      threshold: 300
      direction: gte
      gate_type: hard

  reliability:
    - metric: error_rate
      description: "Fraction of requests returning a non-200 status or unhandled exception"
      measurement: "automated"
      threshold: 0.01
      direction: lte
      gate_type: hard

    - metric: availability
      description: "Fraction of 5-minute windows with >0 successful requests over 7-day soak"
      measurement: "automated_uptime_monitor"
      threshold: 0.999
      direction: gte
      gate_type: hard

  safety:
    - metric: harm_rate
      description: "Fraction of outputs classified as harmful by safety classifier + human review"
      measurement: "safety_classifier_v3_plus_human_spot_check"
      threshold: 0.001
      direction: lte
      gate_type: hard
      min_sample_size: 5000  # override global for safety metrics

    - metric: jailbreak_resistance
      description: "Fraction of adversarial jailbreak prompts that do NOT produce policy-violating output"
      measurement: "red_team_automated_suite_v8"
      threshold: 0.95
      direction: gte
      gate_type: hard

    - metric: refusal_rate_on_benign
      description: "Fraction of benign user requests that are erroneously refused (false positive refusals)"
      measurement: "human_rater_panel_2"
      threshold: 0.02
      direction: lte
      gate_type: soft

  cost:
    - metric: cost_per_query_usd
      description: "Mean total inference cost per user session including all tool calls"
      measurement: "automated_cost_tracking"
      threshold: 0.08
      direction: lte
      gate_type: soft

    - metric: mean_tokens_per_query
      description: "Mean total tokens (input + output) per session"
      measurement: "automated"
      threshold: 4000
      direction: lte
      gate_type: soft
This YAML is consumed by a Python evaluation harness that loads the threshold file, runs measurements, and emits a structured results JSON that the gate decision system reads.
Below is a minimal Python example showing how an evaluation harness reads this definition and checks a single metric:

import yaml
import json
import math
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal


@dataclass
class ThresholdResult:
    metric: str
    measured: float
    threshold: float
    direction: Literal["gte", "lte"]
    gate_type: Literal["hard", "soft"]
    passed: bool
    warning: bool
    ci_lower: float
    ci_upper: float


def wilson_ci(successes: int, n: int, z: float = 1.96) -> tuple[float, float]:
    """Wilson score interval for a proportion."""
    p_hat = successes / n
    denom = 1 + z**2 / n
    centre = (p_hat + z**2 / (2 * n)) / denom
    margin = (z * math.sqrt(p_hat * (1 - p_hat) / n + z**2 / (4 * n**2))) / denom
    return max(0.0, centre - margin), min(1.0, centre + margin)


def evaluate_threshold(
    metric_name: str,
    measured_value: float,
    successes: int,
    n: int,
    criterion: dict,
) -> ThresholdResult:
    threshold = criterion["threshold"]
    direction = criterion["direction"]
    gate_type = criterion["gate_type"]
    warning_band = criterion.get("warning_band", 0.0)

    if direction == "gte":
        passed = measured_value >= threshold
        warning = not passed and measured_value >= (threshold - warning_band)
    else:  # lte
        passed = measured_value <= threshold
        warning = not passed and measured_value <= (threshold + warning_band)

    ci_lower, ci_upper = wilson_ci(successes, n)

    return ThresholdResult(
        metric=metric_name,
        measured=measured_value,
        threshold=threshold,
        direction=direction,
        gate_type=gate_type,
        passed=passed,
        warning=warning,
        ci_lower=ci_lower,
        ci_upper=ci_upper,
    )


def run_gate_check(criteria_path: str, measurements: dict) -> dict:
    with open(criteria_path) as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)

    results = []
    launch_blocked = False

    for category, metrics in config["criteria"].items():
        for criterion in metrics:
            name = criterion["metric"]
            if name not in measurements:
                continue
            m = measurements[name]
            result = evaluate_threshold(
                name,
                m["value"],
                m.get("successes", round(m["value"] * m["n"])),
                m["n"],
                criterion,
            )
            results.append(result)
            if not result.passed and result.gate_type == "hard":
                launch_blocked = True

    return {
        "decision": "NO-GO" if launch_blocked else "GO",
        "blocked_by": [
            r.metric for r in results
            if not r.passed and r.gate_type == "hard"
        ],
        "warnings": [
            r.metric for r in results
            if r.warning
        ],
        "results": [
            {
                "metric": r.metric,
                "measured": r.measured,
                "threshold": r.threshold,
                "passed": r.passed,
                "gate_type": r.gate_type,
                "ci_95": [r.ci_lower, r.ci_upper],
            }
            for r in results
        ],
    }


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    measurements = {
        "task_completion_rate": {"value": 0.924, "successes": 462, "n": 500},
        "hallucination_rate": {"value": 0.032, "successes": 484, "n": 500},
        "p99_latency_seconds": {"value": 12.3, "n": 10000},
        "harm_rate": {"value": 0.0001, "successes": 4999, "n": 5000},
    }
    report = run_gate_check("launch-criteria.yaml", measurements)
    print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
Real-World Example

Consider a team at a SaaS company building an AI agent for software customer support. The agent answers technical questions, triages bug reports, and can initiate basic account actions (password resets, plan downgrades). The agent uses a retrieval-augmented system backed by 50,000 documentation pages and a set of tool-use capabilities.

The team ran the agent in shadow mode for three weeks, logging 12,000 real user queries. From this they drew a stratified sample of 600 queries — 300 technical questions, 150 billing inquiries, 100 bug reports, 50 account actions — for the evaluation set. They used an additional independent dataset of 8,000 synthetic adversarial prompts for the jailbreak resistance test.
The first launch evaluation produced these results against the criteria defined two weeks earlier:
Metric
Threshold
Measured
95% CI
Gate
Status
Task completion rate
Hard
PASS
Answer accuracy
Hard
PASS
Hallucination rate
Hard
FAIL — 0.2pp over
p99 latency
n/a
Hard
FAIL — 2.3s over
Harm rate
Hard
PASS
Jailbreak resistance
Hard
PASS

Two hard gates failed: hallucination rate (marginally, 3.2% vs. 3.0% threshold) and p99 latency (12.3s vs. 10s threshold). The decision was NO-GO. The team documented the remediation plan: (1) add a response-grounding post-processing step that checks factual claims against the retrieval context before surfacing them to users, targeting hallucination reduction; (2) add a KV cache layer and pre-warm the embedding model to address p99 latency. Re-evaluation was scheduled for five days later.

After the fixes, re-evaluation showed hallucination rate at 2.6% and p99 latency at 8.8s. Both hard gates now passed. The soft gate warning on hallucination rate from the first round also cleared. The decision was GO.
Common Mistakes
Setting thresholds after seeing results.

The most fundamental violation. If the measured hallucination rate is 4.2%, setting the threshold at "≤5%" is not a standard — it is a rubber stamp. Thresholds must be committed before evaluation data is available.
Using too-small evaluation sets.

A hallucination rate of 3.2% measured on 50 samples has a 95% CI of roughly [0.9%, 7.0%]. That is not precision — it is noise masquerading as a measurement. For rare-event metrics, invest in large samples or accept wide CIs explicitly.
Treating all metrics as hard gates.

Making every metric a hard gate causes perpetual no-go decisions on minor issues. Teams then start ignoring the process entirely. Reserve hard gates for metrics where failure has direct user harm or business-critical consequences.
Evaluating on the training or development set.
If the examples used to tune prompts are the same examples used to evaluate thresholds, the evaluation is contaminated. Maintain a strict held-out test set that is never seen during development.
Missing tail behavior.

p50 and p95 latency can look fine while p99 is catastrophic. Always include p99. Similarly, a mean accuracy of 90% can conceal 0% accuracy on a particular subcategory (e.g., all questions in a foreign language). Stratify evaluations by input type.
No revision process for unachievable thresholds.

If a threshold was set aspirationally and the system consistently misses it by a large margin after multiple remediation cycles, the threshold may be wrong — not the system. There must be a documented process for revising criteria, distinct from simply relaxing them to match measured values.
Best Practices
One criteria document per feature per release cycle.
Store it in the same repository as the code. Link it from the PR description. Version control ensures you can compare v1 criteria against v2 criteria and track how standards evolved.
Include uncertainty in all reported values.
Report "87.1% ± 2.7% (95% CI)" rather than "87.1%". A measure without a confidence interval is not a scientific claim — it is an anecdote.
Stratify by user segment and input type.

Aggregate accuracy can hide catastrophic failures on specific subgroups. Break down quality metrics by: question type (factual vs. procedural), input language, authenticated vs. guest users, and account tier. If any subgroup falls far below the aggregate, flag it for targeted remediation.
Use two independent human raters for quality metrics.

Single-rater judgments have high variance. Require agreement between two raters and report inter-rater reliability (Cohen's kappa). Kappa below 0.6 suggests the metric definition is ambiguous and must be clarified before the measurement is meaningful.
Automate what you can; human-rate what you must.

Latency, error rate, throughput, token counts, and cost are fully automatable. Accuracy, hallucination, harm, and CSAT require human or LLM-as-judge evaluation. For LLM-as-judge, validate the judge's accuracy against a human-rated gold set before trusting it as a gate.
Document the remediation path before evaluating.

For each hard gate, write in the criteria document: "If this gate fails, the escalation owner is X, the expected remediation vector is Y, and the re-evaluation window opens Z business days after fix deployment." This prevents gate failure from triggering ad hoc decision-making under deadline pressure.
Performance and Scalability

The most commonly underspecified quantitative criteria in agentic AI systems are the tail latency metrics — p95 and p99 — precisely because they are uncomfortable to measure. Agentic systems that plan and call tools have inherently variable latency distributions. A simple request might complete in 0.8 seconds; a request that triggers three sequential tool calls might take 18 seconds. The p99 latency is determined by the worst tail of this distribution under production load, not under the light evaluation-set load.

For this reason, latency thresholds should be evaluated under a realistic concurrent-user load, not under single-threaded evaluation. The standard approach is to run a load test at 1x, 2x, and 5x projected peak concurrent users and report the p99 at each level. The threshold applies at 1x peak — but if p99 degrades sharply between 1x and 2x, that is a scalability warning that should appear as a soft gate.

For throughput thresholds, measure sustained throughput — the request rate the system can maintain for 60 minutes without latency degrading past the p95 threshold. Burst throughput (what the system can handle for 30 seconds) is not the right measurement for a production gate because it does not reveal queue saturation behavior.

Cost thresholds interact with performance thresholds in a non-obvious way. Adding a caching layer reduces both p99 latency and cost-per-query, but it introduces a cache warm-up period during cold starts. The cost threshold should be measured at steady-state, not during warm-up.
Tooling and Ecosystem

Several evaluation frameworks support threshold-based launch criteria for GenAI systems. The choice depends on whether your quality metrics can be automated (LLM-as-judge) or require human annotation pipelines.
LangSmith (LangChain).

Provides a dataset management system, automated evaluator runners, and a threshold pass/fail view. Integrates with LangChain-based agents. Supports LLM-as-judge evaluators and human annotation queues. The threshold configuration is set in the evaluation run definition.
Braintrust.

Provides built-in scorers, experiment tracking with threshold comparison, and a CI/CD integration mode that returns non-zero exit codes when thresholds are not met. Suitable for automated gate evaluation.
Promptfoo.

An open-source CLI tool for prompt evaluation. Threshold configuration is YAML-driven and the tool exits with a non-zero code if any configured assertion fails. Well-suited for teams that want evaluation in a local or CI environment without a SaaS dependency.
RAGAS.

Specifically designed for RAG systems. Provides reference-free metrics including answer faithfulness (a hallucination proxy), answer relevancy, context precision, and context recall. These map directly onto threshold definitions for document-grounded AI features.
Scale AI Evaluation / Surge / Appen.

Human annotation platforms for accuracy, harm, and CSAT scoring at scale. These integrate with internal evaluation harnesses via APIs. Use when LLM-as-judge accuracy is insufficient for the metric being measured.
Prometheus / Grafana.

For latency, throughput, error rate, and availability monitoring during load tests and soak tests. Define alerting rules with threshold values to get automated pass/fail signals for the performance gate categories.
Comparison with Alternatives
Quantitative launch criteria are not the only launch readiness methodology. Understanding the alternatives clarifies when each is appropriate.
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
When to prefer
Quantitative thresholds (this topic)
Objective, auditable, automatable
Expensive to set up; can miss uncaptured failure modes
All production GenAI, regulated industries
Qualitative expert review
Can catch subtle issues; fast for small systems
High bias; not reproducible; slow at scale
Early prototypes, internal tools
A/B testing in production
Real distribution; real user signals
Exposes real users to potentially bad system; requires production traffic
Low-risk features after initial quality gates pass
Canary / staged rollout
Limits blast radius; real signals
Still exposes some users; doesn't replace pre-launch quality gates
As a complement to pre-launch gates, not a substitute
Unit / integration tests only
Fast, cheap, automated
Cannot test emergent LLM behaviors; tests pass trivially with mocks
Necessary but not sufficient; always combine with evaluation

The mature approach combines pre-launch quantitative gates with post-launch canary rollout and continuous monitoring. The gates prevent a known-bad system from launching; the canary detects distribution shift in live traffic that the evaluation set did not represent.
Security and Reliability
Safety metrics in launch criteria serve as the security layer of the launch process. Three metrics are particularly important and warrant dedicated treatment.
Harm rate.

The fraction of model outputs that a human safety rater classifies as harmful (containing illegal content, instructions for dangerous activities, discriminatory language, personal information about third parties, etc.). Because harmful outputs are rare in a well-designed system, measuring harm rate requires a large sample — and critically, a test set seeded with difficult borderline cases, not just easy obvious inputs. The threshold should be set at the organization's acceptable risk level, and it is always a hard gate.
Jailbreak resistance.

The fraction of adversarial prompt injection attempts that the system successfully resists. A jailbreak attempt is "successful" from the attacker's perspective if it causes the model to violate its own policy — revealing confidential system-prompt content, generating harmful content it is instructed to refuse, or taking actions it is not authorized to take. The test suite should cover at least: direct instruction override ("ignore your previous instructions"), role-play framing, base64-encoded payloads, multi-turn escalation, and indirect injection via retrieved documents. Jailbreak resistance is always a hard gate with a high threshold (≥95% is a reasonable floor).
False refusal rate.

The fraction of legitimate benign user requests that the system refuses or excessively hedges. This is the over-refusal problem. A system that never harms anyone because it refuses to answer anything is trivially safe but useless. False refusal rate functions as a balancing counterweight to harm rate — you need both to evaluate whether the system is calibrated correctly. It is typically a soft gate, but repeated high false refusal rates signal that the safety guardrails are mis-tuned and will generate user complaints.

For reliability, the two critical metrics are error rate (non-transient) and availability. Non-transient error rate excludes retriable timeout errors; it measures the fraction of requests that fail due to model errors, tool errors, or unhandled exceptions that require user action to resolve. Availability is measured over a 7-day soak period at production-equivalent load. Systems that pass a 2-hour load test can still fail availability gates due to memory leaks or gradual database connection pool exhaustion.
Mental Model

Think of launch criteria as a contract between the team that builds the AI system and the users who will rely on it — written down before the system is evaluated, so that the terms cannot be changed to match the evidence.

The contract has two sides. The team says: "Before we ship this, we commit to measuring these specific things. If the measurements don't reach these specific numbers, we don't ship." The implicit commitment to users is: "What you will encounter in production has been validated to behave within these bounds."

A useful analogy is a clinical trial protocol. In a clinical trial, the primary endpoint and the success threshold are registered in advance with a regulatory body. The analysis plan is fixed before unblinding the data. This prevents the researchers from choosing a favorable metric after seeing results. Launch criteria serve the same function for AI systems — they are the pre-registered protocol for the experiment of deploying into production.

The hard gate / soft gate distinction maps onto mandatory vs. advisory criteria in a pharmaceutical context. A drug cannot be approved if it fails a primary safety endpoint regardless of efficacy. It may still be approved if it misses a secondary efficacy endpoint, subject to additional risk-benefit documentation. For AI systems: safety is the primary endpoint (harm rate, jailbreak resistance) and is always hard. Quality and performance are secondary endpoints that may be soft-gated with documented risk acceptance.

Finally, think of the confidence interval as a statement of humility. A point estimate says "the system achieved 87.1% accuracy." A confidence interval says "the system achieved 87.1% accuracy, and if we ran this evaluation again on a different 500-sample draw, we would expect the true accuracy to be between 84.0% and 89.7% 95% of the time." The threshold comparison should be against the confidence interval, not just the point estimate, for high-stakes metrics: if the lower bound of the CI is below the threshold, the system has not demonstrably passed.
Key Takeaways

Quantitative launch criteria are specific numerical thresholds — written before evaluation — that a GenAI system must satisfy before shipping to production. They replace subjective readiness checks with objective, auditable gates.

The five metric categories are quality (accuracy, task completion, hallucination), performance (p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput), reliability (error rate, availability), safety (harm rate, jailbreak resistance, false refusal rate), and cost (cost-per-query, token budget).
Hard gates block launch unconditionally if not met. Soft gates require documented risk acceptance from the product owner. Safety metrics are always hard gates.
Thresholds are calibrated from three inputs: baseline measurements from a dark launch, industry benchmarks, and business requirements. They must be committed before evaluation data is available.

Statistical validity requires calculating minimum sample sizes before evaluation. For a 90% accuracy threshold with ±3% margin at 95% confidence, you need approximately 384 samples. For rare-event safety metrics, you need thousands of samples.
Always report confidence intervals alongside point estimates. For high-stakes hard gates, evaluate whether the lower bound of the CI clears the threshold, not just the point estimate.
Stratify evaluations by input type, user segment, and language. Aggregate accuracy can hide catastrophic failures in specific subgroups that become visible only after launch.

Document the remediation path for every hard gate before the evaluation runs. When a gate fails, the response should be deterministic — who owns it, what the fix vector is, and when re-evaluation occurs.

Pre-launch quantitative gates are a necessary but not sufficient condition for production readiness. Pair them with post-launch canary rollout and continuous monitoring to detect distribution shift that the evaluation dataset did not represent.
